audit_status.source_base: Hebrew Judges / Shoftim Jephthah origin, Ammonite negotiation, war, catastrophic vow, and daughter narrative, draft aligned later
audit_status.review_note: First-pass draft reviewed for Jephthah's stigmatized maternal origin, expulsion and return, Gilead headship bargain, diplomatic land-history argument, Spirit-before-vow sequence, vow syntax, daughter-centered grief, sacrifice language, and annual commemoration.

verses.restored: Jephthah the Gileadite was a mighty man of valor, and he was son of a woman who was a sex worker; Gilead fathered Jephthah.
Gilead's wife bore him sons. When the wife's sons grew, they drove Jephthah out and said to him, You shall not inherit in our father's house, because you are son of another woman.
Jephthah fled from before his brothers and dwelt in land of Tob. Empty/reckless men gathered themselves to Jephthah and went out with him.
After days, sons of Ammon fought with Israel.
When sons of Ammon fought with Israel, elders of Gilead went to take Jephthah from land of Tob.
They said to Jephthah, Come, and be for us a commander, so we may fight against sons of Ammon.
Jephthah said to elders of Gilead, Did you not hate me and drive me from my father's house? Why have you come to me now, when distress is upon you?
Elders of Gilead said to Jephthah, Therefore now we have returned to you: you shall go with us and fight against sons of Ammon, and you shall be head for us, for all inhabitants of Gilead.
Jephthah said to elders of Gilead, If you bring me back to fight against sons of Ammon, and YHWH gives them before me, I shall be your head.
Elders of Gilead said to Jephthah, YHWH shall be hearer/witness between us if we do not do according to your word.
Jephthah went with elders of Gilead, and the people set him over them as head and commander. Jephthah spoke all his words before YHWH at Mizpah.
Jephthah sent messengers to king of sons of Ammon, saying, What is there between me and you, that you have come against me to fight in my land?
King of sons of Ammon said to Jephthah's messengers, Because Israel took my land when it came up from Egypt, from Arnon to Jabbok and to Jordan; now return them in peace.
Jephthah again sent messengers to king of sons of Ammon.
He said to him, Thus says Jephthah: Israel did not take land of Moab or land of sons of Ammon.
For when they came up from Egypt, Israel walked in wilderness as far as Sea of Reeds and came to Kadesh.

Israel sent messengers to king of Edom, saying, Please let me pass through your land, but king of Edom did not listen. They also sent to king of Moab, but he was unwilling, so Israel stayed at Kadesh.

They walked in wilderness, went around land of Edom and land of Moab, came from east of sun toward land of Moab, and camped across Arnon. They did not enter border of Moab, because Arnon was border of Moab.
Israel sent messengers to Sihon king of Amorite, king of Heshbon. Israel said to him, Please let us pass through your land to my place.
But Sihon did not trust Israel to pass through his border. Sihon gathered all his people, camped at Jahaz, and fought with Israel.
YHWH, Divine of Israel, gave Sihon and all his people into hand of Israel, and they struck them. Israel possessed all land of Amorite inhabiting that land.
They possessed all border of Amorite, from Arnon to Jabbok and from wilderness to Jordan.
And now YHWH, Divine of Israel, has dispossessed Amorite from before his people Israel; and you would possess it?
Will you not possess whatever Chemosh your god causes you to possess? And whatever YHWH our Divine has dispossessed from before us, that we will possess.
Now are you really better than Balak son of Zippor, king of Moab? Did he ever contend with Israel, or did he ever truly fight against them?
While Israel dwelt in Heshbon and its daughter-towns, in Aroer and its daughter-towns, and in all cities beside Arnon three hundred years, why did you not recover them in that time?
I have not sinned against you, but you are doing evil with me by fighting against me. May YHWH the Judge judge today between children of Israel and sons of Ammon.
King of sons of Ammon did not listen to words of Jephthah that he sent to him.
Spirit of YHWH came upon Jephthah. He passed through Gilead and Manasseh, passed through Mizpeh of Gilead, and from Mizpeh of Gilead he crossed toward sons of Ammon.
Jephthah vowed a vow to YHWH and said, If giving, you give sons of Ammon into my hand,
then the one who comes out, who comes out from doors of my house to meet me when I return in peace from sons of Ammon, shall belong to YHWH, and I will bring that one up as an ascending offering.
Jephthah crossed toward sons of Ammon to fight against them, and YHWH gave them into his hand.
He struck them from Aroer until you come to Minnith, twenty cities, and as far as Abel-cheramim, a very great blow; sons of Ammon were subdued before children of Israel.
Jephthah came to Mizpah, to his house, and look—his daughter came out to meet him with tambourines and dances. She alone was his only child; apart from her he had neither son nor daughter.
When he saw her, he tore his clothes and said, Ah, my daughter! Bringing low, you have brought me low, and you have become among my troublers. I have opened my mouth to YHWH, and I cannot turn back.
She said to him, My father, you have opened your mouth to YHWH; do to me according to what went out from your mouth, after YHWH has made vengeance for you from your enemies, from sons of Ammon.
She said to her father, Let this thing be done for me: release me for two months, and I will go and descend upon the mountains and weep over my virginity—I and my companions.
He said, Go, and sent her away two months. She went, she and her companions, and wept over her virginity upon the mountains.
At end of two months she returned to her father, and he did to her his vow that he had vowed. She had not known a man. It became a statute/custom in Israel:
From days to days, daughters of Israel would go to commemorate/lament daughter of Jephthah the Gileadite four days in the year.

translation_notes: Verse 1 preserves the stigmatizing label zonah for Jephthah's mother. Her occupation or social classification does not diminish her humanity or make Jephthah morally defective.
Jephthah is expelled from inheritance by his brothers and later recruited by the same leadership during crisis; the headship bargain exposes opportunistic use of an outcast person.

Jephthah first attempts extended diplomacy. His historical argument distinguishes Ammonite and Moabite claims, though verse 24's reference to Chemosh as Ammon's god is historically and textually difficult.
The Spirit of YHWH comes upon Jephthah in verse 29 before he makes the vow. The vow is not required to secure Divine presence or victory.

Verses 30-31 use explicit vow and ascending-offering language. Attempts to make the vow harmless by default are not secure; the narrative must be read under Torah's rejection of child sacrifice and its provisions for evaluating or redeeming vows.
Jephthah's statement that he cannot turn back is his claim, not a Divine command. A vow cannot create a righteous duty to abuse or kill another person.
Verse 35 shifts blame onto the daughter for a danger created entirely by Jephthah. The translation preserves the accusation so its victim-blaming can be recognized and rejected.

The daughter is never named, but she speaks, requests time, gathers companions, and becomes the center of a women's memorial practice. Her consent cannot be inferred from speech produced inside paternal, military, religious, and social coercion.
Virginity language marks the loss of her own future, relationships, descendants, and bodily autonomy; it does not make sexual inexperience a superior spiritual state.
Verse 39's wording, 'he did to her his vow,' remains terse and horrifying. The source layer should not erase the likely death, while noting the history of alternative dedication readings.
Letannot in verse 40 may carry senses such as recount, commemorate, praise, or lament. The annual gathering preserves the daughter's memory rather than celebrating Jephthah.
safety_note: Maximum safeguard: Jephthah's vow is catastrophic, abusive, and incompatible with any ethic that protects children. Do not praise it as faith, obedience, sacrifice, masculine honor, or spiritual courage. Do not blame his daughter, infer free consent, demand that victims honor another person's vow, or use this chapter to justify child sacrifice, self-sacrifice, coercive promises, honor violence, forced celibacy, or religious control. The women who remember her provide the chapter's clearest counter-memory.
